title: Why Latin?
summary: Why the Church uses Latin and why it matters.
history: Latin became the language of the Roman Church in the 3rd-4th centuries, replacing Greek as the common language of the Western Empire. The Roman Canon of the Mass, the core of the liturgy, has been prayed in Latin for at least 1,600 years. For most of Church history, Latin was the universal language of the Western Church — a priest from Portugal could celebrate Mass in Japan, and the faithful could follow along identically. The Second Vatican Council did not abolish Latin; Sacrosanctum Concilium (1963) stated that 'the use of the Latin language is to be preserved in the Latin rites.'
explanation: Latin serves several theological and practical purposes in the liturgy: (1) Universality — it is no one's native language, making it equally accessible to all nations. (2) Precision — Latin's fixed theological vocabulary prevents doctrinal drift through translation ambiguity. (3) Sacred character — a language set apart from daily use elevates the mind to the sacred. (4) Continuity — the same words prayed by Augustine, Aquinas, and countless saints connect us across centuries. (5) Unity — Catholics worldwide share a common liturgical language. Latin is not an obstacle to participation; it is an invitation to deeper interior prayer.
practice: Begin by learning the responses at Mass: 'Et cum spiritu tuo,' 'Amen,' 'Deo gratias.' Follow along with a hand missal that has Latin on one side and English on the other. Pray the Pater Noster and Ave Maria in Latin daily. Over time, the prayers become deeply familiar. The goal is not to 'understand' Latin as you would a conversation, but to pray it — to let the ancient words carry your prayer to God.
churchTeaching: Second Vatican Council, Sacrosanctum Concilium §36: 'The use of the Latin language is to be preserved in the Latin rites.' Pope John XXIII, Veterum Sapientia (1962): 'Of its very nature Latin is most suitable for promoting every form of culture among peoples.'

title: Ecclesiastical Latin Pronunciation
summary: How to pronounce Latin as it is spoken in the Church.
history: Ecclesiastical (Church) Latin pronunciation differs from Classical Latin as taught in secular universities. The Ecclesiastical pronunciation developed in Italy and became the standard for the Roman Church. Pope St. Pius X formally mandated it for liturgical use in 1903 with the motu proprio Tra le sollecitudini. The key differences from Classical pronunciation reflect centuries of Italian phonetic influence on how Latin was spoken in Rome and throughout the Church's liturgical life.
explanation:
The major rules of Ecclesiastical pronunciation:

'ae' and 'oe' are pronounced 'ay' (as in 'they'), not 'eye' as in Classical Latin. 'Ave' is 'AH-vay,' not 'AH-wye.'

'c' before 'e,' 'i,' 'ae,' or 'oe' is soft, like 'ch' in 'church.' 'Caelum' is 'CHAY-loom.' Before other vowels or consonants, 'c' remains hard like 'k.'

'g' before 'e' or 'i' is soft, like 'j' in 'gentle.' 'Regina' is 'ray-JEE-nah.'

'v' is always 'v' (as in 'vine'), never 'w' as in Classical Latin.

'ti' before a vowel is pronounced 'tsee.' 'Gratia' is 'GRAH-tsee-ah.'

'gn' is pronounced like 'ny' in 'canyon.' 'Agnus' is 'AHN-yus.'

Double consonants are both pronounced distinctly. 'Ecce' is 'ET-chay,' not 'EH-kay.'
practice: The best way to learn Ecclesiastical pronunciation is to listen and repeat. Attend the Traditional Latin Mass and follow the priest's pronunciation. Use the phonetic guides in this app's prayer section, where stressed syllables are marked in red. Start with short prayers (Sign of the Cross, Glory Be) and work up to longer ones. The pronunciation will become natural with daily practice.
churchTeaching: Pope St. Pius X, Tra le sollecitudini (1903), established Ecclesiastical pronunciation as the norm for all liturgical Latin.

title: Essential Latin Vocabulary
summary: The most frequently used Latin words in prayers and the Mass.
history: The vocabulary of the Latin liturgy is surprisingly compact. A relatively small number of words appear repeatedly across prayers, the Ordinary of the Mass, and hymns. Learning these core words provides a foundation for understanding the liturgy without needing to study Latin grammar formally. Many of these words have entered English directly or through French, making them more familiar than they first appear.
explanation:
Core liturgical vocabulary:

Deus (God), Dominus (Lord), Christus (Christ), Spiritus Sanctus (Holy Ghost), Maria (Mary), Pater (Father), Filius (Son), Mater (Mother)

Sanctus (holy), beatus (blessed), omnipotens (almighty), aeternus (eternal), misericors (merciful)

Ora/orate (pray), credo (I believe), confiteor (I confess), laudamus (we praise), adoramus (we adore), deprecamur (we beseech)

Peccatum (sin), gratia (grace), gloria (glory), pax (peace), lux (light), vita (life), mors (death), salus (salvation), caelum (heaven)

Nobis (to us/for us), tuus/tua (your/thy), noster/nostra (our), meus/mea (my)

Et (and), in (in), cum (with), pro (for), ad (to/toward), per (through), qui (who), quia (because), sed (but), nunc (now), semper (always), amen (so be it)
practice: Start by identifying these words as you follow Mass in your missal. Underline them lightly. Within weeks, you will begin to recognize them instantly and understand the general sense of prayers without needing the English translation. This is not about academic study — it is about letting the Church's language become your own through repeated, prayerful exposure.

title: Responses at Mass
summary: The Latin responses the faithful give during the Traditional Mass.
history: In the Traditional Latin Mass, the server responds on behalf of the congregation. However, the faithful are encouraged to make the responses interiorly and, in a Dialogue Mass (Missa Dialogata), aloud. Pope St. Pius X and Pope Pius XII both encouraged the faithful's active participation through understanding and joining in the responses. The Dialogue Mass, formally approved by the Sacred Congregation of Rites in 1958, allows the congregation to recite certain responses together with the server.
explanation:
The essential responses:

Priest: 'Dominus vobiscum' (The Lord be with you)
Response: 'Et cum spiritu tuo' (And with thy spirit)

Priest: 'Oremus' (Let us pray)
Response: Interior assent

Priest: 'Per omnia saecula saeculorum' (World without end)
Response: 'Amen'

Priest: 'Sursum corda' (Lift up your hearts)
Response: 'Habemus ad Dominum' (We have lifted them up to the Lord)

Priest: 'Gratias agamus Domino Deo nostro' (Let us give thanks to the Lord our God)
Response: 'Dignum et iustum est' (It is meet and just)

End of readings: 'Deo gratias' (Thanks be to God)
End of Gospel: 'Laus tibi, Christe' (Praise be to Thee, O Christ)

Dismissal: 'Ite, Missa est' (Go, the Mass is ended)
Response: 'Deo gratias' (Thanks be to God)
practice: Memorize 'Et cum spiritu tuo,' 'Amen,' and 'Deo gratias' first — these three responses cover most of the Mass. Then learn 'Habemus ad Dominum' and 'Dignum et iustum est' for the Preface dialogue. Practice by following along in your missal and saying the responses quietly. At a Dialogue Mass, join in with the congregation. Even at a Low Mass, say the responses interiorly.
churchTeaching: Pope Pius XII, Mediator Dei (1947) §105: 'All the faithful should be aware that to participate in the Eucharistic Sacrifice is their chief duty and supreme dignity.'
